serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Japanese milk pudding cup, approximately 120 g, as typically served in a Japanese dessert restaurant or café.

nutrient_source.title: Pudding, vanilla, prepared from recipe, with whole milk — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Japanese milk pudding (purin) is a milk-and-egg-based set dessert closely analogous to vanilla pudding prepared with whole milk. USDA SR Legacy entry for vanilla pudding prepared from recipe with whole milk (FDC ID 169113) is used as the primary nutrient reference and adapted to reflect the typical Japanese purin formulation, which uses whole milk, sugar, eggs, and a setting agent (gelatin or agar). Values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, and dietary fibre are drawn from this source. Micronutrient values are carried where applicable.

gi_source.title: Milk, full fat, fresh, pasteurised — Glycemic Index Database; and custard/milk pudding analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Japanese milk pudding (purin) was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 43 based on the analogous food 'milk pudding / custard' category. Whole-milk-based custard and milk pudding entries in the University of Sydney GI Database report GI values in the range 35–47 (e.g., full-fat milk GI ~27, vanilla pudding with milk GI ~43–47). A conservative mid-range estimate of 43 is applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (43 × 19.2) / 100 ≈ 8.3, rounded to 8.2 for 120 g serving (carbohydrate_g per serving = 16.0 × 1.2 = 19.2 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~75 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Japanese milk pudding contains approximately 75 mg sodium per 100 g, which is moderate; individuals with hypertension may consume it occasionally in standard single-cup portions without significant sodium concern, but should account for it within daily sodium limits of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content (~13.5 g per 100 g) and added sugar from recipe
type2_diabetes.guidance: Japanese milk pudding contains approximately 13.5 g of sugar per 100 g with a low estimated GI of 43; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to one small cup (approximately 120 g) and account for approximately 19 g of carbohydrate per serving within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (~75 mg per 100 g) from whole milk and eggs
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Japanese milk pudding provides approximately 2.1 g saturated fat and 75 mg cholesterol per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small servings and prefer lower-fat milk variants where available to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Japanese milk pudding, known in Japanese as ミルクプリン (Miruku Purin) or simply プリン (Purin), is a smooth, silky, chilled dessert made primarily from whole milk, sugar, and eggs, set with gelatin or agar to achieve a soft, wobbly texture. It is a popular Japanese confection served in cafés, dessert restaurants, and convenience stores across Japan and in Japanese cuisine establishments worldwide. The texture is delicate and creamy, with a mild, sweet, milky flavour that distinguishes it from heavier Western-style custard puddings. Japanese milk pudding is typically served cold in individual cups or moulds, sometimes topped with caramel sauce, fruit, or whipped cream. Nutritionally, a standard 120 g serving provides approximately 133 kcal, 4.2 g protein, 4.6 g fat, and 19.2 g carbohydrate, with a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 43, making it a relatively moderate-impact dessert compared to high-sugar confections. The dessert contains dairy and eggs and is not suitable for vegans or those with dairy or egg allergies. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, portion control is advised due to the sugar content. The sodium content is low to moderate, posing minimal concern for hypertension when consumed in standard single-cup portions. Japanese milk pudding is a culturally significant dessert in Japanese cuisine and is widely enjoyed as an after-meal sweet or afternoon snack.

Is Japanese milk pudding suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact match for Japanese milk pudding (purin) was found in a primary regional approved source such as HPB Singapore Foodo or a Japanese national food composition database accessible for this record. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA SR Legacy entry for vanilla pudding prepared from recipe with whole milk, which is a closely analogous milk-and-egg-based set dessert. GI is estimated from analogous milk pudding and custard entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169113 (vanilla pudding, prepared from recipe, whole milk) as the closest approved-source analogue for Japanese milk pudding (purin). The Japanese purin formulation is closely comparable: whole milk, sugar, eggs, and a setting agent. Trans fat is 0.0 g as confirmed negligible in whole-milk dairy pudding per USDA data. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g as confirmed by USDA source for milk-based pudding with no fibre-contributing ingredients. Vitamin C is 0.0 g as naturally zero in a milk-and-egg-based dessert with no fruit or vegetable components. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin A, B vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are carried from the USDA SR Legacy source. Fields set to -1 are not applicable here as all reported fields carry numeric values from the adapted source. GI estimated at 43 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous milk pudding/custard category (GI range 35–47 for whole-milk-based custard and pudding). Glycemic load per serving = (43 × 19.2) / 100 = 8.3 g (reported as 8.2 after rounding). Serving size of 120 g is based on typical single-cup portion for Japanese milk pudding as served in Singapore Japanese restaurants. The input record references GyoGyo - Funan (field survey data) at SGD 7.85; this pricing and restaurant data are preserved in context but do not influence nutrient values. The food_id GD004148 is preserved from the input seed record.
